brief.title: Best Supplements for PCOS: Evidence-Based Stack (2026)
brief.description: Inositol, NAC, vitamin D, and magnesium all have meaningful PCOS evidence. Here's which ones actually move insulin, cycles, and fertility — and at what doses.
key_points: Inositol (myo + D-chiro, 40:1 ratio, 2-4g daily) — strongest PCOS evidence. Unfer 2017 meta-analysis showed improved insulin sensitivity, ovulation, and cycle regularity
NAC (1.5-3g daily) — improved ovulation rates in PCOS vs placebo (Fulghesu 2002). Also supports oral glucose tolerance
Vitamin D (2000-5000 IU D3) — PCOS patients frequently deficient. Correction improves menstrual regularity per several RCTs
Magnesium glycinate (200-400mg) — addresses common deficiency, supports insulin sensitivity + reduces anxiety common in PCOS
Omega-3 (1-2g EPA+DHA) — reduces androgens and improves lipid profile in PCOS (Sadeghi 2017)
Berberine (500mg 3x/day) — metformin-like effects on insulin; useful when metformin isn't tolerated. NOT to combine without doctor supervision
Spearmint tea (not a supplement but evidence-supported for androgen reduction) — briefly mention
What NOT to take: high-dose biotin (skews TSH labs), cinnamon extract (weak evidence for PCOS specifically despite marketing), excess iodine (can worsen thyroid dysregulation common in PCOS)
Protocol consideration: introduce one at a time, track symptoms (cycle length, hirsutism, weight, mood) for 3 months per addition

brief.title: Best Supplements for Menopause: What Actually Works
brief.description: Hot flashes, bone loss, sleep disruption, and mood changes — menopause is multi-system. Here's the evidence-based stack that addresses each without HRT.
key_points: Vitamin D3 + K2 (2000-5000 IU D3 + 90-180mcg MK-7) — critical for bone density post-menopause. Cosman 2014 meta-analysis supports supplementation
Calcium (500-1000mg, paired with K2) — dose DEPENDS on dietary intake; excess without K2 increases arterial calcification risk
Magnesium glycinate (300-400mg) — sleep, anxiety, and bone co-factor. Most perimenopausal/menopausal women deficient
Creatine (3-5g) — NOT just for athletes. Chilibeck 2017 showed reduced bone density loss in postmenopausal women + improved lean mass
Omega-3 (1-2g EPA+DHA) — cardiovascular protection as estrogen drops, plus mood support (EPA-dominant)
Ashwagandha (KSM-66, 600mg) — mood, sleep, and cortisol modulation during the vasomotor-symptom years
Black cohosh — specific evidence for hot flashes (Leach 2012 Cochrane), but quality and dosing matters. Modest effect
Soy isoflavones — mixed evidence, more effective in women with genetic equol-producer status
What NOT to take: phytoestrogens indiscriminately (estrogen-sensitive cancer survivors should avoid), high-dose evening primrose (minimal evidence), maca root (limited RCT data for hot flashes)
Coordinate with healthcare provider — supplements complement but don't replace decisions about HRT

brief.title: Best Supplements for Fatty Liver (NAFLD): What the Evidence Shows
brief.description: NAC, vitamin E, omega-3, and milk thistle all have NAFLD evidence — but quality varies wildly. Here's which actually reduce liver enzymes and fat.
key_points: Vitamin E (800 IU/day in NASH) — PIVENS trial showed histological improvement in non-diabetic NASH patients. Careful: doses >400 IU have cardiovascular caveats
NAC (1200mg daily) — improves liver enzymes (ALT/AST) in NAFLD per multiple small RCTs. Replenishes hepatic glutathione
Omega-3 EPA+DHA (2-4g) — reduces liver fat content on MRI per meta-analyses (Parker 2012). Works through reduced hepatic de novo lipogenesis
Milk thistle (silymarin) — mixed evidence; better when standardized. Gillessen 2020 meta-analysis showed ALT reduction in NAFLD
Berberine (500mg 3x) — improves lipid profile + reduces hepatic steatosis markers in human trials
Vitamin D (if deficient) — common deficiency in NAFLD patients, correction may help but not standalone treatment
Coffee (not a supplement but solid evidence for liver health) — note separately
Weight loss, exercise, and dietary pattern are primary — supplements are adjunctive, not replacement
What NOT to take: high-dose iron without confirmed deficiency (liver is iron-sensitive), high-dose niacin for cholesterol (can worsen transaminases)
Monitor LFTs at baseline + 3 months when starting any stack

brief.title: Best Supplements for ADHD and Focus: Evidence-Based Stack
brief.description: Omega-3, L-theanine, and magnesium have real ADHD focus evidence. Here's how they work, what they don't replace, and stacking with or without stimulants.
key_points: Omega-3 EPA-dominant (1-2g, high EPA ratio) — strongest evidence. Chang 2018 meta-analysis showed modest but significant improvement in inattention scores
L-theanine (200-400mg) — reduces hyperactivity and improves attention in ADHD children (Lyon 2011). Safer than caffeine for hyperarousal
Magnesium glycinate (200-400mg) — low magnesium correlates with ADHD severity. Starobrat-Hermelin 1997 showed symptom improvement in deficient kids
Zinc (15-30mg) — some positive trials, especially in zinc-deficient ADHD patients. Bilici 2004 showed improvement
Iron (if ferritin <30ng/mL) — low ferritin linked to ADHD severity. Don't supplement without confirmed deficiency
Vitamin D — many ADHD patients deficient. Correction may help, not a standalone
Creatine (5g daily) — emerging cognitive evidence (Avgerinos 2018), particularly under sleep deprivation
L-tyrosine (500-1000mg) — precursor to dopamine. Anecdotal support but limited RCT evidence for ADHD specifically
Lion's mane — NGF mechanism, emerging cognitive evidence
Stacking with stimulants: generally safe but watch magnesium with amphetamines (can alter absorption timing)
What NOT to take: Adderall-alternative 'stimulant' products with yohimbine or high caffeine, mega-dose B-vitamins without deficiency

brief.title: Best Supplements for Runners: What Actually Helps Endurance
brief.description: Iron, electrolytes, beta-alanine — runner-specific supplements with real evidence. Plus the ones runners waste money on (looking at you, BCAAs).
key_points: Iron (if ferritin <30ng/mL) — runners commonly iron-deficient (foot-strike hemolysis, GI bleeding). Check BEFORE supplementing
Electrolytes (Na, K, Mg) — not Gatorade. For runs >60min, especially in heat. Precision Hydration model or DIY
Creatine (3-5g) — YES for runners. Improves repeated sprint performance, glycogen storage, recovery between hard sessions
Beta-alanine (3.2-6.4g/day) — improves buffering capacity, particularly 400m-2mi pace work. Tingling is normal (paresthesia)
Caffeine (3-6mg/kg pre-run) — strongest ergogenic aid in all of sport science. Works for endurance via glycogen sparing + CNS stimulation
Nitrate (beetroot juice, 6-12 mmol pre-event) — reduces oxygen cost of submaximal running by 1-3%. Meaningful over marathon distance
Omega-3 — anti-inflammatory for recovery, cardiovascular protection
Vitamin D — affects bone stress fracture risk. Check levels
Protein adequate daily intake (1.4-1.8g/kg) — whey or casein for post-run recovery
BCAAs: skip. If your daily protein is adequate, BCAAs add nothing (Morton 2018 meta-analysis)
Glutamine: skip. Claims don't hold up in RCTs
HMB: narrow evidence, mostly for novice lifters, not endurance

brief.title: Best Supplements for Perimenopause: The Pre-Menopause Protocol
brief.description: Perimenopause starts up to 10 years before menopause. The supplement needs are different — and most guides conflate the two. Here's the perimenopause-specific stack.
key_points: Perimenopause = 4-10 years BEFORE final period. Distinct from menopause — hormones are fluctuating, not low
Magnesium glycinate (300-400mg) — sleep disruption is the most common early perimenopause symptom. Glycinate for sleep + mood
Vitamin D3 + K2 — start BEFORE menopause for bone density. Correction takes months
Omega-3 (1-2g EPA+DHA) — hormone fluctuations increase inflammation markers. EPA for mood stability
B-complex (B6, B12, folate methyl forms) — energy support during hormonal volatility. Methyl forms for MTHFR variants
Ashwagandha (KSM-66, 600mg) — mood swings and cortisol spikes common in perimenopause. Doesn't affect hormones directly, supports HPA axis
Creatine (3-5g) — bone and muscle mass preservation BEFORE menopause changes. Smith-Ryan 2021 evidence extends here
DIM (diindolylmethane, 100-200mg) — estrogen metabolism support. Some evidence for symptom relief in estrogen-dominant perimenopause
Chasteberry (Vitex agnus-castus, 20-40mg extract) — progesterone support for irregular cycles and PMS worsening in perimenopause
What NOT to take: bioidentical progesterone cream without medical guidance, high-dose phytoestrogens in personal/family history of hormone-sensitive cancer
Track symptoms: HOT flashes, sleep, mood, cycle length. Supplements alone won't manage severe symptoms — discuss HRT options with provider
